item.title: Activist wins environment award
item.description: BOUGAINVILLE’s environment and human rights activist Theonila Roka Matbob is among the five winners of the 2026 Goldman Environment Prize in the United States. The awards ceremony for the Goldman Environmental Prize, also known as the Green Nobel Prize, was held at the San Francisco Opera House in San Francisco.
item.content:encoded:
BOUGAINVILLE’s environment and human rights activist Theonila Roka Matbob is among the five winners of the 2026 Goldman Environment Prize in the United States.

The awards ceremony for the Goldman Environmental Prize, also known as the Green Nobel Prize, was held at the San Francisco Opera House in San Francisco.

“The move taken was completely new to world. Such has never been done anyway, thus people who followed the process nominated me. I had no idea,” Matbob said.

Matbob, from Makosi Village in Panguna district, began her campaign during her final year at the Divine Word University in 2013, pushing Rio Tinto to take responsibility for the environmental and human rights damage left behind at the Panguna copper mine.

Rio Tinto opened the mine in 1972 and was in operation for 17 years.

During that time, mine wastes were dumped into rivers. Farmland and fisheries were destroyed. Villagers suffered health problems.

The company left in 1989 after a civil war broke out, caused by the damage the mine had on the environment and the people.

The war lasted 10 years, and killed more than 15,000 Bougainvilleans –including Matbob’s father.

She became the lead complainant in a 2020 case filed by the Human Rights Law Centre, accusing Rio Tinto of human and environmental rights violations linked to the mine.

She also won a seat in Bougainville’s House of Representatives to extend her advocacy.

Her persistence eventually brought Rio Tinto back to the table.

In 2024, the company committed to addressing the mine’s lasting impacts and funded an independent assessment – a highly unusual move for a mining company.

“It gives me hope to keep going, and its relieving to say the actual reason why Bougainville became deeply devastated has finally caught the world’s attention,” she said.

Matbob added that the fear of being condemned had long silenced Paguna people from speaking out and her team had to go out into the communities to explain the process before they agreed to sign.

In her speech at the awards ceremony, Matbob acknowledged her husband Nathan Matbob, legal director Karen Adams of the Human Rights Law Centre, and the staff at Sage Fund who nominated her for the award.

Matbob announced at the ceremony that she would be travelling to Australia within two weeks to meet Rio Tinto’s new chief executive at the company’s annual general meeting.

She called on supporters around the world who attended the ceremony to sign a petition through the Goldman Environmental Prize website.

Despite the challenges, Matbob said she would not stop fighting.

“As long as I am alive, I won’t give up because it’s my life and the future generations’ life,” she said.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Primary school receives 1,250 books
item.description: EXCITEMENT filled the air at the Ted Diro Primary School in Port Moresby yesterday when students received 1,250 books to help in their education. The contribution is part of the library re-establishment programme led by the Buk bilong Pikinini (BbP), to support school libraries in the country.
item.content:encoded:
EXCITEMENT filled the air at the Ted Diro Primary School in Port Moresby yesterday when students received 1,250 books to help in their education.

The contribution is part of the library re-establishment programme led by the Buk bilong Pikinini (BbP), to support school libraries in the country.

The donation was made possible through a K32,000 sponsorship by Blue Water Shipping. It will cover seven school library kits.

The primary school received 1,000 books while the elementary school was allocated 250.

Head teacher Cecilia Kelgai explained that the school, which serves more than 3,000 students, does not have a separate building for a library.

“Although we do not have a library building, our teachers are trying their best to set up their libraries in the corners of their classrooms and encouraging our students to read,” Kelgai said.

She thanked Buk bilong Pikinini on behalf of the students and teachers.

Mary Sawa, BbP’s public relations and marketing team leader, said the books were sourced from donors in Australia and shipped to PNG via their team in Canberra.

The programme supports urban and rural schools, with PNG Air providing free freight for kits sent outside of Port Moresby.

Sawa encouraged more businesses to get involved, stating that for corporate houses with programmes supporting education, they should reach out to Buk bilong Pikinini as many schools remain in need.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Bank warns citizens not to involve in crypto
item.description: THE Bank of Papua New Guinea (BPNG) is warning members of the public to stop being involved in crypto or fast-money schemes. “BPNG does not license any Virtual Asset Service Providers (VASPs), cryptocurrency-based investment platforms, or fast-money schemes,” bank governor Elizabeth Genia said.
item.content:encoded:
THE Bank of Papua New Guinea (BPNG) is warning members of the public to stop being involved in crypto or fast-money schemes.

“BPNG does not license any Virtual Asset Service Providers (VASPs), cryptocurrency-based investment platforms, or fast-money schemes,” bank governor Elizabeth Genia said.

“Scams often promise extremely high returns in short timeframes, such as turning K30,000 into K270,000 in 28 hours or offering 400 per cent returns.

“These entities are not authorised or supervised by the BPNG, meaning participants have no recourse if they lose money.

“Fraudsters often use forged documents, forged signatures of the BPNG governor, and the bank’s official letterhead to claim they are transferring funds held in ‘offshore-accounts’ or from pre-independence holdings.

“Scammers commonly request upfront fees or payments to release or verify funds.”

The recent complaint was about an alleged scammer who has been on the run after taking millions of Kina from more than 5,000 individuals he had lured into investing in a company called BidAsk/Tanglik Finance Investment Company.

The person identified by the Bank of South Pacific Financial Group Limited and police was using the name “Job Piner”, operating out of Jup Centre Building in Port Moresby.   

He allegedly promised 400 per cent interest per month on members’ investment money.

Complainant Isaac Kipoi claimed that he invested more than K9,000 and had been waiting for the return on his investment.

The bank has advised that the company account number under the name of Joe Piner has only K26 in it and still active.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Democracy: Government of the people, by the people, for the people
item.description: Democracy is not a new idea. It goes back more than 2,000 years to ancient Greece. The word comes from a combination of the words ‘demos’ meaning ‘common people’ and ‘kratos’ meaning ‘rule’ or ‘strength’. Demokratias, therefore mean ‘people rule’.
item.content:encoded:
Democracy is not a new idea. It goes back more than 2,000 years to ancient Greece.

The word comes from a combination of the words ‘demos’ meaning ‘common people’ and ‘kratos’ meaning ‘rule’ or ‘strength’. Demokratias, therefore mean ‘people rule’.

The best known words used to describe democracy were spoken by a United States President, Abraham Lincoln, in 1863 when he said democracy was “government of the people, by the people and for the people”.

Democracy is not just about voting, getting a member into parliament and getting goods and services in return: it is about a system of government which requires respect for the individual, freedom of speech and the equal treatment of all people by the legal system.

Democracy can be achieved directly by the people by letting everyone have their say.

That sometimes happens among small groups when a meet -ing is being held and every member of the group has the opportunity to attend, speak and vote. 

By way of example, for a long time in the Enga region, decisions were made by clansmen in the kabuingi that was held in the men’s house. 

Under that system, men gathered and discussed issues such as tribal wars, rituals and compensation. 

Each adult male was expected to speak, listen and weigh up the arguments until a consensus (or agreement) was reached.

Adult men enjoyed complete freedom of speech while the boys only sat and listened.

 The kabuingi is an example of direct democracy although it must be noted that the women were not included.

However, for a country with millions of people living in many different places, it is not possible to have that system. 

That is why democratic countries usually have not a direct form of democracy but an indirect form of democracy.

Democracy is achieved indirectly when representatives are chosen in free and fair elections and where all adult men and women vote and their individual votes count equally. 

Clearly, democracy requires freedom of the press and freedom of political expression so that people can be informed about the candidates who are seeking to represent the people.

Democracy is not majority rule because if that was the case then the majority could oppress or ignore the minority. 

A democratic government will have ways in which the rights of minority rights are respected.

 It is important to note that, under democracy, when someone wins a seat in a parliament, he or she does not represent only the people who voted for him or her but for all the people in that electorate, including all those who did not cast their votes for him or her and those too young to vote.

There are alternatives to democracy. 

They include a dictatorship, which is when one person makes all the decisions.

Another alternative to democracy is when a country is ruled by people who have not been elected, such as when a country is run by its army. 

In 1975, when the Independent State of Papua New Guinea began, it adopted a Constitution which provides for democracy as the form of government.

When an election is held, the country is divided into areas called electorates.

 An electorate is also called a seat because the person who wins the election in an electorate gains a seat in the National Parliament.

Previously, voters in PNG only voted for one candidate. 

However, under that system there were occasions when there were so many candidates that the winning candidate had less than 10 per cent of the votes. 

Although that candidate was the winner, the reality was that 90 per cent of the voters in that area did not want that candidate to represent them! 

That is why a system called limited preferential voting was introduced.

Full preferential voting would require that voters put a number on the ballot paper for every candidate. 

In other words, if there are 30 candidates then it would be necessary to use the numbers from one to 30.

item.title: Omenefa champions leadership
item.description: RUTHA-Meu Omenefa, winner of the sports and arts category of the Westpac Outstanding Women’s Award (WOWA) 2025, believes that coaching, while building athletes, should also shape confident and value-driven individuals. “The greatest impact for me is seeing young players come forward seeking advice, applying what they have learnt in their lives, and growing as leaders among their peers,” she said.
item.content:encoded:
RUTHA-Meu Omenefa, winner of the sports and arts category of the Westpac Outstanding Women’s Award (WOWA) 2025, believes that coaching, while building athletes, should also shape confident and value-driven individuals.

“The greatest impact for me is seeing young players come forward seeking advice, applying what they have learnt in their lives, and growing as leaders among their peers,” she said.

For Omenefa, success is measured not only in sporting achievements but in the growth of young people in school and their communities.

She said leadership is grounded in service and sacrifice, with sport serving as a platform to instill confidence and values.

“As a coach, youth mentor and community leader, I give my time, trust and encouragement to ensure my players are equipped not just for competition but for life,” she explained.

Reflecting on her recognition as last year’s WOWA winner, Omenefa said the award has given her renewed drive to inspire more young women in rugby league.

“The award opened doors, strengthened trust and gave me a greater responsibility to prove myself through the work I do,” she said.

Westpac Pacific managing director Emma Low praised Omenefa’s story as embodying the purpose of the WOWA programme.

“Rutha-Meu exemplifies leadership that creates lasting impact, not only through sporting achievements but through service, mentorship and values-based leadership.”

Westpac PNG chief executive Andrew Cairns added that her journey sends a powerful message nationwide.

“Women leading in sports and the arts are shaping confidence, unity and creating opportunities for the next generation.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Duadak sparks win
item.description: PAPUA New Guinea’s youngest striker Delma Duadak sealed the deal for her side in their first OFC Under-16 Women’s Championship Qualifying campaign on home soil with an 8-2 victory over American Samoa yesterday at the PNG Football Stadium. Duadak, 14, from Malmal district in Madang, stepped onto the playing field with simple instructions from coach Susan Steven where she followed effortlessly to ge
item.content:encoded:
PAPUA New Guinea’s youngest striker Delma Duadak sealed the deal for her side in their first OFC Under-16 Women’s Championship Qualifying campaign on home soil with an 8-2 victory over American Samoa yesterday at the PNG Football Stadium.

Duadak, 14, from Malmal district in Madang, stepped onto the playing field with simple instructions from coach Susan Steven where she followed effortlessly to get PNG’s eighth goal.

Coach Steven commended Duadak for sticking to the game plan even though she did not get much game time, also Oscalina Gunemba for getting her two goals in while Emeliah Khanna held down the midfield throughout.

PNG asserted control from the outset, dominating possession and creating a wave of early chances with attacker Chriszanne Sifas from Rai Coast in Madang, who got her hat-trick in this match before Gunemba stepped in with her two goals to extend PNG’s lead in the first half.

American Samoa struggled to relieve the pressure, pinned deep in their half as PNG continued to control the tempo and leading with 6-0 into halftime.

American Samoa returned for the second half with renewed energy and intent, showing a much stronger presence in attack and enjoying more time in the PNG defense-third.

Their improvement was rewarded in the 48th-minute when Abigail Lam Sam scored her team’s first goal of the tournament, her effort taking a deflection on its way into the net.

The goal lifted their confidence, as they continued to press forward and challenge the hosts more effectively than in the opening period.

Salvation Taula was able to pull back another for American Samoa, but a late own goal restored PNG’s five-goal lead to quash any hopes of a comeback.

Duadak would have the final say for PNG, adding their eighth, when breaking through on goal and smuggling the ball past the keeper.

The result has eliminated American Samoa from contention and sets up a winner-takes-all encounter between PNG and Cook Islands on Friday.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: School rugby league kicks off in Kundiawa
item.description: THE Chimbu Academic School Rugby League kicked off on Friday at Kundiawa’s Dickson Oval. Governor Noah Kool, alongside members of the Simbu provincial rugby league development (SPRLD) board, launched the programme in what is seen as a major step forward for youth development in Chimbu.
item.content:encoded:
THE Chimbu Academic School Rugby League kicked off on Friday at Kundiawa’s Dickson Oval.

Governor Noah Kool, alongside members of the Simbu provincial rugby league development (SPRLD) board, launched the programme in what is seen as a major step forward for youth development in Chimbu.

The initiative will involve both boys and girls, promoting inclusivity while nurturing young talent across Chimbu.

The provincial education division has formally endorsed the programme, allowing schools to participate in this key pathway.

Importantly, the school rugby league programme is a requirement for re-entry into the Digicel ExxonMobil Cup, making it a vital part of the province’s sporting ambitions.

The launch has already sparked strong interest from schools eager to join, signalling a promising future for rugby league in Chimbu.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: American Samoa exit early
item.description: AMERICAN Samoa were eliminated from the OFC Under 16 Women’s Championships qualifying stage after consecutive defeats to Cook Islands (0-10) on Saturday and Papua New Guinea (2-8) yesterday at the PNG Football Stadium in Port Moresby. Coach Diane Justus reflected on the PNG match, noting the team’s intent to rebound after their opening loss.
item.content:encoded:
AMERICAN Samoa were eliminated from the OFC Under 16 Women’s Championships qualifying stage after consecutive defeats to Cook Islands (0-10) on Saturday and Papua New Guinea (2-8) yesterday at the PNG Football Stadium in Port Moresby.

Coach Diane Justus reflected on the PNG match, noting the team’s intent to rebound after their opening loss.

“The game plan to attack early in the first half did not work out, but the girls showed fighting spirit and managed two goals early in the second half,” she said.

She explained that fatigue and heat hindered their finish, but the halftime switch to a more attacking formation created opportunities that surprised PNG.

“It’s about gaining more experience on the international stage. The girls have a long way to go in their football careers, and they need these strong challenges to grow,” she added.

American Samoa have scheduled a friendly against the Hekari Academy girls’ team on Friday to gain more game time.

Meanwhile, Papua New Guinea coach Susan Steven expressed frustration that her players did not fully follow instructions despite the win.

“The two goals came in the second half due to substitutions and silly mistakes in front of goal,” she said.

Looking ahead, she expects a tough contest against Cook Islands on Friday.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Action to save communities
item.description: UNREGULATED alluvial mining along the Bulolo River in Bulolo and Wau-Waria is placing communities at risk. Activities by foreign and local companies, often in partnership with landowners, but without clear strategy or oversight, are affecting vital access routes – particularly a footbridge relied upon by students, mothers and elderly in Ward One and Ward Nine.  This is a call to action.
item.content:encoded:
UNREGULATED alluvial mining along the Bulolo River in Bulolo and Wau-Waria is placing communities at risk.

Activities by foreign and local companies, often in partnership with landowners, but without clear strategy or oversight, are affecting vital access routes – particularly a footbridge relied upon by students, mothers and elderly in Ward One and Ward Nine.  This is a call to action.

Failure to repair damaged roads and protect access points could lead to serious consequences, including environmental degradation and harm to already vulnerable communities.

There is nothing wrong with landowners benefitting from resources on their land.

However, poorly managed mining – especially river diversion and sediment disruption – threatens livelihoods and safety.

Some residents without mining leases have already seen homes and gardens damaged or washed away. All leaseholders, including those of ML165 and ML554, must work with investors, communities and regulators to agree on a responsible path forward.

Proper consultation and safety measures are non-negotiable.

If immediate steps are not taken, this matter will be formally escalated to the Mineral Resources Authority and the Conservation and Environment Protection Authority. Collaboration is essential – development must not come at the cost of people and the environment.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Introduce grade-skipping
item.description: PAPUA New Guinea is wasting some of its most capable students by applying a rigid, one-size-fits-all education system. In many classrooms, Grade Six students are already working at Grade Eight level, yet are forced to wait.
item.content:encoded:
PAPUA New Guinea is wasting some of its most capable students by applying a rigid, one-size-fits-all education system.

In many classrooms, Grade Six students are already working at Grade Eight level, yet are forced to wait.

This creates boredom, disengagement, and, in some cases, behavioural issues that lead to early dropout.

The result is a loss of national talent at a critical stage.

A structured grade acceleration, or “grade-skipping”, policy would address this.

It is not about rushing children, but matching learning with ability.

Gifted students could advance in subjects like math and science while remaining with their age group for social development.

Countries such as Singapore, Australia, and the United States already use academic acceleration to help high-performing students’ progress faster and achieve earlier entry into university and the workforce.

PNG would need safeguards: proper testing, teacher training, parental consent, and psychologist assessment to ensure readiness.

A pilot programme across provinces could help measure outcomes before national rollout.

The benefits are significant – reduced dropout rates, lower education costs for families, and faster development of skilled professionals.

Most importantly, it ensures that talent is not lost simply because the system moves too slowly.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Nation needs honest and strong leadership
item.description: PAPUA New Guinea is at a decisive turning point. There is a growing call for leaders, particularly from the Southern, Momase, and the New Guinea Islands regions, to lead with integrity, fairness, and discipline in the true spirit of nationhood.
item.content:encoded:
PAPUA New Guinea is at a decisive turning point.

There is a growing call for leaders, particularly from the Southern, Momase, and the New Guinea Islands regions, to lead with integrity, fairness, and discipline in the true spirit of nationhood.

Since independence, these regions have been associated with principled leadership, but many citizens now fear that tribalism, money politics, and self-interest are undermining national progress.

Despite over K150 billion in public spending in recent years, basic services remain poor and public trust continues to decline.

This is not a call for division, but for reform.

Leaders must reject corruption, nepotism, and electoral manipulation, and uphold constitutional governance in the national interest.

PNG deserves leadership that serves the people with honesty and dignity.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Phillie fine-tuning leadership qualities
item.description: WHEN Phillie Julai Marai traveled to Hawaii in 2022 for the United States Young Pacific Leaders Conference, she had just graduated from university and was beginning her career. “It was my first year working. I was looking for opportunities to learn, grow and understand how I could contribute more effectively to my community.”
item.content:encoded:
WHEN Phillie Julai Marai traveled to Hawaii in 2022 for the United States Young Pacific Leaders Conference, she had just graduated from university and was beginning her career.

“It was my first year working. I was looking for opportunities to learn, grow and understand how I could contribute more effectively to my community.”

Phillie, 30, from Kokopo in East New Britain, returned home with a broader regional perspective, a strengthened network and a renewed commitment to serve her community

She was one of four Papua New Guineans selected to attend the conference – a leadership initiative sponsored by the US States Department for emerging leaders from the Pacific to discuss education, leadership development and community engagement.

Phillie had just graduated from Divine Word University in Madang with a Bachelor in International Relations degree before joining Tribal Foundation, a PNG-based non-governmental organisation.

“The experience really opened my mindset. Being with young leaders from the Pacific helped me understand what others were doing and what I could take back home to improve my work.”

It was the second overseas event she attended. She had earlier, while still at university, attended a governance and human rights workshop in Fiji.

Her interest was on education. She had even started a small private school in her home province.

“I was really drawn to education. I wanted to connect with educators and leaders who were investing in learning and see how we could support each other.”

Her regional networking reinforced the importance of collaboration beyond national borders.

“The Pacific is diverse, but many of our challenges are shared. That realization stayed with me.”

After returning home, Phillie ensured that she passed on what she learnt at the conference to young people during organised workshops.

She also applied for a small grant through the Young Pacific Leaders programme. With the grant, she established a community library at her school in East New Britain. The facility now serves students and community members.

“That was something tangible I could give back. It was important to make sure the opportunity created something sustainable.”

She is the country director for Senisim PNG, a leadership development programme under the Tribal Foundation.

We work with people from all walks of life who want better leadership and positive change. I ensure those opportunities reach as many people as possible.”

Senisim meaning “change” in Tok Pisin, focuses on promoting values-based leadership and strengthening ethical decision-making in communities and organisations.

Her role includes overseeing programme implementation, building partnerships and expanding leadership training opportunities nationwide.

“We work with people from all walks of life who want better leadership and positive change. I ensure those opportunities reach as many people as possible.”

Phillie also serves as chief executive officer of the Pacific Australia Youth Association, a regional organisation supporting youth-led initiatives in the Pacific.

“The YPL network became my professional network. Those connections created bridges for young people and open doors for regional collaboration.”

What she learned at the Young Pacific Leaders Conference continues to shape her career, not only as a leader but also as a mentor for the next generation.

It helps emerging leaders from Pacific Island countries by strengthening their leadership capacity, fostering regional cooperation and promoting people-to-people ties across the Pacific.

Through conferences, professional exchanges and alumni engagement, the programme encourages young leaders to address challenges and contribute to positive change in their communities.

Phillie encourages young professionals who already hold leadership roles to consider applying for the Young Pacific Leaders Conference.

“There are individuals who already have influence. With the right support and exposure, they can come back and make a meaningful impact on their communities.”

Her message to young people is simple: “Leadership starts with you. Change begins when you are willing to change your own behaviour, values and mindset. That’s how transformation happens.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PNG elections: The time to worry was then
item.description: There are irregularities, allegations, omissions, bad decisions and tragedies. The problems are so bad that, for a brief moment, the country’s political elites take notice, organise a review, launch the review and then – with the occasional exception – appear to lose all interest.
item.content:encoded:
There are irregularities, allegations, omissions, bad decisions and tragedies.

The problems are so bad that, for a brief moment, the country’s political elites take notice, organise a review, launch the review and then – with the occasional exception – appear to lose all interest.

Donors also get involved, get thwarted, get distracted (did someone say China?) and then hope, optimistically, that today’s small projects will eventually deliver improvements.

Then the next election arrives – with a bang – and problems aplenty.

Part of the answer will be familiar to anyone who has put off anything ever: time.

Too much of it. PNG’s elections occur at five-yearly intervals.

That’s long enough for urgent problems to feel like tomorrow’s problems.

The trouble is that, if you want future elections to be better, you need to start planning today.

Building a new, accurate roll, in particular, takes years.

Electoral reform ought to start in the immediate aftermath of electoral failure.

Another part of the answer is that miracle cures – usually biometric voting or electronic counting – are very tempting, particularly when touted by foreign companies with slick sales pitches.

Although technology may be tempting, it’s no substitute for the dreary work required to run elections well.

Other than making the electoral process more complex, biometric voting won’t help if the roll is a historical artefact.

And, although it’s a slow process, thanks to the presence of scrutineers, ballot counting is actually pretty good in PNG.

Good enough that the last thing anyone should want to do is introduce a complex and opaque new technology to replace it.

The clientelist nature of Papua New Guinea’s politics also contributes to the problem.

Clientelism leads to MPs focusing more on patronage, rather than national issues.

General elections are, by their very nature, a national issue and so they receive too little attention.

This structural lack of political will means that the sustained domestic effort required to improve electoral quality is always lacking, all the more so because poorly run elections actually help some MPs when it comes time to contest their seats.

Then there’s donors – or to be more specific – donor.

In PNG, most donor countries aren’t large enough to tackle major challenges such as elections.

In 2023, according to Lowy Institute Pacific Aid Map data, it gave more money to PNG than all other donors combined.

But despite the fact that it spends something in the vicinity of A$700 million (about K2.2 million) in aid a year in Papua New Guinea, and despite the importance of elections, it never seems to engage comprehensively.

Don’t get me wrong: individually there are some genuine experts involved. (Full disclosure: I currently have a minor role as part of a team providing Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade with some advice on elections globally, but my role is minor, and the expertise I’m talking about here isn’t mine.)

And Australia also provides very valuable logistical support close to the time of the election.

But it’s one thing to have experts and help at the last minute.

It’s another thing to devote substantial attention and money over time.

It’s true that Australia, as an external actor, is constrained by the extent to which Papua New Guinea’s government requests assistance, but it still has some agency.

Australian involvement will never be a panacea. But enduring, fully focused attention from Australia would nudge things in the right direction.

Too much time, wishful thinking about technical quick fixes, uninterested politicians, inadequately determined donors – mix all these ingredients together and you have an explanation as to why we’re here in 2026 with PNG’s next election scheduled for next year and so little has been achieved.

item.title: Inside smoky shelters, a fast-paced, illegal card game emerges
item.description: AS the school day ends in Honiara, *Irene, a 43-year-old teacher in a floral dress with a yellow daisy in her bun, steps on to a minibus. After 10 minutes, Irene gets off the bus, walks down an alley, and enters a damp, smoky shelter.
item.content:encoded:
AS the school day ends in Honiara, *Irene, a 43-year-old teacher in a floral dress with a yellow daisy in her bun, steps on to a minibus.

After 10 minutes, Irene gets off the bus, walks down an alley, and enters a damp, smoky shelter.

Plastic tables fill the space and playing cards are scattered on the floor.

Irene has stopped by a hidden gambling table in a western suburb of Honiara to play Pass, a street card game gaining popularity in the Solomon Islands capital.

There are dozens of these games dotted across the city, with new sites appearing regularly.

Authorities are trying to stamp out the games as Solomon Islanders – young, old, low-income or salaried professionals – are drawn to Pass for their chance at a big payout, while risking big losses.

Dealers attract players by shouting out the price of the bet: “$20 down!”

Players are dealt seven cards, and the dealer tables a number six card.

The first player must put down a five or a seven, and the pattern continues, with each player having to play the next sequential card.

If they can’t, they yell “pass!” The first person to get rid of all their cards wins.

The winner collects the pot each round, save for one bet held as the dealer’s fee.

With up to 30 rounds per hour, large sums are won or lost in minutes.

“I don’t have money left, but I’ve asked the kids to run me some,” Irene tells the Guardian , after losing several hands in a row.

The single mother discourages her three children from playing.

“Gambling is a bad thing. Sometimes, other people who don’t have money steal from mothers,” she says, while closely watching the cards being dealt.

But Irene has no plans to give up because, she says, her livelihood depends on it.

Despite the evening’s losses, she was up SBD$500 (about K240) for the week, a sum that nearly matches her US$600 (about K2,600) teacher’s salary.

Irene typifies those flocking to play Pass.

Earning a meagre income in the formal economy, she sees it as a way to make enough money to support her family.

Facing a lack of opportunities, many young Solomon Islanders see the game as a way to get ahead.

*Ben is the 19-year-old dealer on Irene’s table.

He started dealing Pass as a 15-year-old to cover his school fees.

For his boss, Pass is much more lucrative.

“We make SBD$12,000 (about K5,650) per week on this table,” says 29-year-old *Gordon, who supplies cigarettes and betel nut, a local intoxicant, at no charge to his loyal gamblers.

Collectively, his three tables turn over SBD$30,000 (about K16,240) each week.

For others, like *Madlyn, 29, Pass is a social game.

She plays every night at the same table.

“I just won!” she proclaims, as the setting sun beams across the table, before receiving a packet of cigarettes and two meals wrapped in aluminium foil.

Phillip Subu, a prominent youth advocate, sees Pass as a symptom of Solomon Islands’ deep economic malaise.

“It’s getting out of hand because a lot of people here in Honiara don’t have employment. The biggest cause is unemployment,” Subu said.

item.title: Two-Mile Hill and Port Moresby’s housing crisis
item.description: The dust may now have settled at Two-Mile Hill, but 1,200 former residents of Port Moresby’s Rabiagini settlement remain homeless. They are the unfortunate victims of the latest of Papua New Guinea’s state-sponsored eviction campaigns.
item.content:encoded:
The dust may now have settled at Two-Mile Hill, but 1,200 former residents of Port Moresby’s Rabiagini settlement remain homeless.

They are the unfortunate victims of the latest of Papua New Guinea’s state-sponsored eviction campaigns.

Forced to move into other crowded settlements scheduled for future demolition, they embody Port Moresby’s interlocking crisis of housing insecurity, escalating violence and failing urban policy.

Urban squatter settlements are growing rapidly in the cities of Australia’s Melanesian neighbours.

Across Noumea, Suva, Port Vila and Honiara, each is a testament to the region’s accelerating rural-urban migration.

The issue is most pronounced in PNG’s capital Port Moresby – where over 50 per cent of the city’s population now reside informally and illegally in sprawling squatter settlements.

These agglomerations emerged in response to ongoing housing shortages and reconnect people who felt separated from friends and family when they reside in culturally unfamiliar Western-style suburbs.

Communities recreated village structures on unoccupied stretches of state-owned land, wiring up their own (usually illicit) power connections and tapping into the city’s water.

They were first called “self-help” settlements: as settlers had no choice but to help themselves establish basic services.

Now, settlements are a defining feature of Port Moresby.

They are equally the primary target of National Capital District (NCD) Governor Powes Parkop’s urban policy.

Political elites, the Royal PNG Constabulary and many others have long blamed settlements for Port Moresby’s manifold urban problems – from the deteriorating city water supply to carjacking.

Traditional media and increasingly pervasive social media amplify this narrative.

Settlements are often stereotyped as hotbeds of criminality and settlers, by association, as hordes of unwelcome criminals.

This rhetoric exacerbates existing settler marginalisation.

While trustworthy quantitative data is scarce in PNG, qualitative markers strongly suggest Port Moresby residents face record unemployment.

When a supermarket opened recently at Eight-Mile, a large settlement in the city’s north, and advertised entry-level positions, owners were met by a queue of 3,000 hopefuls –many waiting more than 12 hours in the sun and without food, for the possibility of an interview.

Without income, settlers cannot secure loans necessary to access formal housing.

They remain trapped in the settlements under the ever-present threat of violent eviction.

Despite this insecurity, many settlements have grown into communities of thousands.

My previous research combining satellite imagery analysis with ground-level interviews suggests Eight-Mile, a settlement covering 250 hectares, houses around 25,000 persons, many of whom have lived there for decades.

Their houses are far from shanties, built not just to last but for children to inherit.

However, children growing up in a settlement cannot legally inherit this land.

Instead, they will inherit housing insecurity.

The informal (and often traditional) network of leases governing settlement housing are not accepted by PNG’s legal system.

Despite official acknowledgement that customary land leasing is widely understood and accepted, the NCD recognises only those physically possessing land titles, which grow increasingly valuable.

Land titles in a fast-growing city are sensible capital investments for corporations – particularly so when security is provided by the state.

This dynamic has generated, and continues to generate, sporadic violent confrontations between settlers and officials executing evictions.

Typically, settlers receive 30 days’ notice before police, private security and bulldozers arrive to burn and level the settlements.

This may be ordered pre-emptively by the NCD or at the behest of a land-title holder.
